other_observers: None. However, I spoke on the phone with Steve Mlodinow during the period of observation.; Yes. No other observers at the time I was there.
description: First, I note that an earlier written description appears here: http://tinyurl.com/7vwbc9k  I first saw the bird in the presence of a "Thiceland" Gull, i.e., a gull with characters intermediate between Thayer's and Iceland. Like the "Thiceland" Gull, the bird in question was a "large white-headed gull" (LWHG), but definitely on the dainty side for an LWHG. In particular, the bird at rest on the water appeared more buoyant than would a larger LWHG. In direct comparison with nearby Ring-billed Gulls, the bird was clearly larger.  Sitting on the water, the bird was notably pale/white, especially on the exposed wingtips. The mainly or entirely black bill was small and thin for an LWHG.  On several occasions, the bird engaged in leisurely, low, soaring, circling flight, providing prolonged views of the upper wing surfaces. Most notable were the uniform, quite-pale-gray wingtips, concolor with the clearly visible secondary bar.Behaviors: On several occasions, the bird picked at the water's surface. Of course, any gull can do this, but I consider that behavior to be consistent with the overall "dainty" build of Iceland--relative to other LWHGs.  The bird alternately swam/floated on the water's surface and engaged in low, mainly soaring flights close to shore.  I'm unclear as to whether the bird can be said to have "kept company" with the other LWHGs that were out there. Initially, it consorted with the "Thiceland" Gull, but then it seemed to do its own thing.Call: none heard.Plumage: fairly late first cycle
